summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #4 by sharing DuckDB's explosive adoption metrics, including 5 million monthly extension installs, half a million unique website visitors, and a rise to #45 on the DB-Engines ranking. He announces the upcoming 0.10 release and outlines the path to 1.0, which will follow a 'Snow Duck' philosophy inspired by Apple's Snow Leopard—focusing entirely on stability with no new features. The centerpiece of the 1.0 vision is backwards-compatible database file format, which Hannes positions as a potential new de facto standard for storing tabular data. Mark Raasveldt then details 0.10's features including multi-database attach for Postgres/MySQL, a 3x faster CSV reader, fixed-length arrays, unified memory manager, ALP floating point compression, and storage format improvements.
key_points: DuckDB has reached 5 million monthly extension installs, generating 2TB/day of download traffic, with half a million unique website visitors
The 1.0 release follows a 'Snow Duck' strategy: a series of bug-fix-only releases focused purely on stability and robustness, with no new features
Backwards compatibility of the database file format is the flagship 1.0 feature, positioning DuckDB files as a potential de facto standard for tabular data storage
DuckDB Labs operates without VC funding, requiring deliberate prioritization of support efforts and now offering commercial support contracts
The 0.10 release includes multi-database attach (Postgres, MySQL), 3x faster CSV reading, fixed-length arrays, unified memory manager, and ALP compression
money_quotes: Little did we know that this would create a ginormous Amazon bill, because we are now running at something like two terabytes a day of extension downloads.
We are just very grateful to Cloudflare to not charge us for the traffic.
I think we accidentally made one of the more popular websites in this country.
We are not VC-funded, which means we don't have 50 million sitting in a bank account that we can spend on a 50-people DevRel team.
We just want to make sure that this is a DuckDB release you can deploy on a satellite and fly to Venus without having to worry about patching your DuckDB instance halfway through.
We think we have a pretty interesting shot at becoming a new sort of de facto format to store tabular data in a somewhat efficient way.
I want to say that there may be some of our so-called competitors that spend more time on their websites than we do, because we spend more time on making the system good.
If you want to learn more about these, you can talk to me, because we also don't have a giant sales team. We have Hannes.
key_insight: DuckDB's path to 1.0 prioritizes stability and backwards-compatible file format permanence over new features, aiming to become the de facto standard for storing tabular data.
